medical.title: Blacks, Hispanics and Asians use asthma inhalers less than whites, study finds
medical.summary: Despite guidelines recommending daily controller inhalers as the best treatment for asthma, new UCLA-led research finds that Blacks, Hispanics and Asians use them less than whites, suggesting that socioeconomic factors and lack of access to specialty care are at the root of this gap.

medical.title: Simple blood test could lead to personalized lung cancer treatment
medical.summary: A single blood test could help doctors predict how lung cancer patients will respond to treatment before therapy begins, researchers have found. University of Queensland-led research focused on non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC), the most common form of the disease, and showed how analyzing proteins in a blood sample could support earlier and better-informed treatment decisions.

medical.title: New studies describe how immune modulation can effectively combat Valley Fever
medical.summary: Valley Fever, technically known as Coccidioidomycosis, is a dust-borne fungal infection that occurs in dry regions like the southwestern US and is proliferating in California and Arizona. California alone spends $1 billion a year on treatment.

medical.title: Natural trans fats in dairy do not raise heart disease risk
medical.summary: Trans fats found naturally in dairy foods such as milk, butter, and cheese do not increase the risk of heart disease or type 2 diabetes, a new study has found. Researchers analyzed evidence from 22 studies involving thousands of people across Europe, Canada, and the United States and found that natural trans fats behave very differently in the body from the industrial kind. Unlike industrial trans fats, which are strongly linked to heart disease, those found in dairy appear to pose no risk.

medical.title: Planes, trains and pandemics: Lessons from COVID‑19 about travel risks posed by hantavirus and Ebola
medical.summary: International travel volumes have now fully recovered from the downturn during the COVID-19 pandemic. With the upcoming World Cup poised to drive a surge in Canadian tourism, recent hantavirus and Ebola virus outbreaks remind us of the need to effectively manage travel-related public health risks in a world on the move.

medical.title: Stress before conception may reprogram sperm and boost male offspring growth
medical.summary: Research from the University of Colorado Anschutz suggests that stress experienced by a father before conception may influence an offspring's growth by altering small molecular signals in sperm. The study, published in iScience, focuses on a tiny stress-responsive molecule called let 7f 5p, found in sperm. This molecule increases under stress. Researchers found that it may help shape how an embryo develops in its earliest stages, which can later influence growth after birth.

medical.title: Studies expose hidden toll of trauma recovery in NZ
medical.summary: Researchers from the University of Otago—Faculty of Medicine—Christchurch Ōtautahi are calling for improved long-term psychological support for survivors of major trauma after landmark studies reveal many patients in Aotearoa New Zealand are struggling to access appropriate follow-up care.

medical.title: Human monoclonal antibodies fight antimicrobial resistance during disease treatment
medical.summary: The overuse of antibiotics is increasingly leading to the emergence of infectious superbugs—dangerous bacteria that have developed antimicrobial resistance (AMR) and are therefore much harder, if not impossible, to eliminate from the body. In a review published in Trends in Immunology, researchers highlight the promise of using human monoclonal antibodies (mAbs) to treat patients more effectively and tackle AMR.

medical.title: Extreme trait values may trace to rare genes with outsized effects, analysis suggests
medical.summary: Researchers at the Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai have found evidence that people who fall at the extreme high or low ends of certain traits, such as cholesterol, blood glucose, height, and age at menopause, are more likely to have a simple genetic explanation than previously thought.

medical.title: Long COVID may affect 18 million Americans, doubling surveillance estimates
medical.summary: The true toll of long COVID may be double that of current estimates and hidden from current surveillance systems that rely on capturing diagnostic codes, according to new research led by Mass General Brigham. Investigators have used a novel AI algorithm to comb through medical records of nearly 460,000 patients with COVID-19 across 58 U.S. hospitals, finding that approximately one in six (roughly 16%) developed long COVID.

medical.title: Years after polyp removal, gut microbiome changes may still shape colorectal cancer risk
medical.summary: More than a decade after removal of an adenoma—a precancerous mass—from the colon, alterations to the gut microbiome and metabolites remain and may drive heightened risk of colorectal cancer (CRC), according to a study led by researchers at Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health. The work is published in Cell Host & Microbe.

medical.title: Study links eight heart-healthy habits to lower diabetes risk in postmenopausal women
medical.summary: Type 2 diabetes is often associated with excessive sugar intake, but lifestyle factors including body weight, exercise, and smoking habits can also influence risk. A new study published in Diabetology has found that greater adherence to eight habits for cardiovascular health is associated with lower rates of diabetes for postmenopausal women.

medical.title: Probiotics may help prevent childcare gastrointestinal bugs
medical.summary: New research suggests probiotics could help reduce "gastro bugs" in children attending childcare and kindergartens—potentially easing the familiar cycle of gastro lurgies for parents and families.

medical.title: How inflammation can worsen artery plaque and also hold it back
medical.summary: A new LMU study shows how different immune cells variously influence the formation of dangerous vascular deposits—and identifies miR-147 as a potential starting point for future therapies.

medical.title: KRAS degradation induces rapid lung cancer regression in preclinical mouse models
medical.summary: KRAS is one of the oncogenes most frequently altered in cancer, mutating in approximately one-third of lung adenocarcinomas. For decades, it was considered undruggable, until the recent approval of the first inhibitors against specific KRAS mutants. However, these treatments often lose efficacy over time due to the emergence of resistance. Now, a new study explores a different pharmacological strategy based on inducing tumor cells to degrade mutant KRAS themselves.

medical.title: Climate change linked to rising antibiotic resistance in Salmonella
medical.summary: Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) is mainly driven by the overuse and misuse of antibiotics, which allows resistant bacteria to survive and spread. However, rising temperatures and changing rainfall patterns can influence how bacteria survive, mutate, and spread, potentially increasing the exchange of antibiotic resistance genes. While previous studies have linked higher temperatures to greater levels of resistant bacteria, global quantitative studies on this relationship have been limited.

medical.title: Daily fruit juice may lower depression scores in four weeks, trial suggests
medical.summary: People who drink a glass of 100% fruit juice or a smoothie each day as part of the U.K.'s 5-a-day healthy eating guidance see improvements in their mental well-being, according to new research from Newcastle University, U.K.

medical.title: Strength exercises improve young people's hip pain
medical.summary: Physiotherapist-led strength exercises improve hip pain in young people suffering hip joint impingements, new research shows. The La Trobe University study followed 154 participants over six months, comparing a targeted strengthening program with a standardized stretching program. Participants in the strength group were 2.3 times more likely to report perceived improvements in pain and had larger improvements in hip muscle strength compared to participants in the stretch group.

medical.title: Why you wake up so tired after vivid dreams
medical.summary: Some mornings when you wake up, your head is fuzzy, your body is heavy, and you don't feel rested. It felt like you were dreaming all night. But did all that dreaming actually wear you out? Let's look at what the science says.

medical.title: New laser heat treatment could stop blindness before it starts
medical.summary: A new experimental treatment could finally offer hope for millions of people with dry age-related macular degeneration — one of the leading causes of blindness in older adults. Researchers at Aalto University discovered a way to gently heat tissue at the back of the eye using near-infrared light, triggering the cells’ natural “cleanup and repair” systems before major damage occurs.

medical.title: The English restaurant turning hospitality on its head
medical.summary: At a pay-as-you-can restaurant in Stroud, radical hospitality and good food are bringing strangers together The post The English restaurant turning hospitality on its head appeared first on Positive News .

medical.title: The world’s largest museum of illustration is about to open its doors
medical.summary: British illustrator Quentin Blake has established a new museum dedicated solely to his craft, aiming to uplift other artists The post The world’s largest museum of illustration is about to open its doors appeared first on Positive News .

medical.title: Surprising study finds beef doesn’t worsen blood sugar or diabetes risk
medical.summary: A new clinical trial suggests that eating beef every day may not be as risky for people with prediabetes as many assume. Researchers found that adults who ate 6–7 ounces of beef daily for a month showed no worsening in blood sugar control, insulin function, inflammation, or other key markers linked to type 2 diabetes when compared to people eating poultry instead.

medical.title: Scientists discover simple way to relieve arthritis pain without pills or surgery
medical.summary: A surprisingly simple walking tweak may offer new hope for millions living with knee osteoarthritis. In a year-long clinical trial, researchers found that slightly changing the angle of a person’s foot while walking reduced knee pain as effectively as common medications — and even slowed cartilage damage inside the joint.

medical.title: Scientists uncover surprising health benefits of watermelon
medical.summary: Studies suggest watermelon could be a hidden powerhouse for better health. Researchers found that people who eat watermelon tend to have higher-quality diets packed with more vitamins, fiber, and antioxidants — while consuming less added sugar and saturated fat. Another study showed watermelon juice may help protect blood vessel function and support heart health.

science.title: The strange quantum property of tomorrow's insulator
science.summary: Ultra-fast data transfer and superconductivity: Quantum materials offer significant technological prospects—if we can understand them at the atomic scale. A team from the University of Geneva (UNIGE), in collaboration with the University of Salerno, the Institute of Materials Science of Barcelona, and the National Research Council of Italy, has succeeded in observing the "quantum metric" in a topological insulator—a unique geometric property of these materials, which conduct electricity only on their surface.

science.title: La NASA anunciará la tripulación de Artemis III e informará sobre el progreso de la misión
science.summary: La NASA informará sobre los avances de la misión Artemis III de la agencia y anunciará los astronautas asignados a este vuelo de prueba durante un evento en vivo a las 11 a.m. EDT (hora del este) del martes 9 de junio en el Centro Espacial Johnson de la agencia en Houston. Siga la rueda […]

science.title: The impact of nanoplastics on neurons may depend on their size
science.summary: Smaller plastic particles have more effects on neurons, the key information processing cells of the brain, new research from the University of Eastern Finland shows. In the study, neuronal cells were exposed to polystyrene nanoplastics at low doses to study subtle changes.

science.title: Women's faces outrank men's in attractiveness across cultures, global study shows
science.summary: Why are women considered the "fairer sex" in humans, when in most animal species it is males that display the more elaborate and visually striking traits? This question has intrigued researchers since Darwin. A new large-scale study led by the Max Planck Institute for Empirical Aesthetics (MPIEA) in Frankfurt now provides clear empirical evidence for the first time. The findings have just been published in Proceedings of the Royal Society B.

science.title: Students Build Moon Robots for NASA’s 2026 Lunabotics Challenge
science.summary: Katherine Rauscher of Michigan Technological University prepares her team’s prototype lunar robot for its turn during the finals for NASA’s 2026 Lunabotics Challenge competition on Tuesday, May 19, 2026, at the Kennedy Space Center Visitor Complex in Florida. Forty-seven teams from around the U.S. designed and built remote-controlled robots capable of traversing challenging lunar terrain while […]

science.title: Advancing detection of genome-edited crops in food mixtures
science.summary: Researchers from Sciensano, partner of the DARWIN project, have published a new paper in npj Science of Food addressing one of the key scientific and regulatory challenges linked to genome-edited (GE) organisms, their reliable detection and identification in complex food mixtures.

science.title: Webinar 6/17: Discover, Access, and Task Commercial Data with NASA’s Satellite Data Explorer
science.summary: Learn how to use the Satellite Data Explorer to search, access, and task commercial Earth Observation data.

science.title: Coral study could help explain infertility and ovarian cancer by decoding cilia-driven fluid flows
science.summary: A study by researchers at The University of Manchester, carried out alongside the Universities of Melbourne and Copenhagen, could hold the key to understanding the causes of long-term health problems, such as infertility and ovarian cancer.

science.title: Droplet-like clusters reveal new control switch for major drug-target receptors
science.summary: A study by researchers at Duke University School of Medicine identifies a new way that G protein–coupled receptors (GPCRs)—targets of roughly one-third of FDA-approved drugs—control signaling in cells. The paper is published in the journal Nature.

science.title: NASA’s Webb Reveals Black Hole That Formed Before Its Galaxy
science.summary: Which comes first, the galaxy or the black hole? We don’t know, but scientists have long thought it could be the galaxy: Large stars within an existing galaxy consume their fuel and collapse to form black holes, which can gobble up surrounding material and merge over time to form more massive entities. But it’s hard […]

science.title: Fish have no necks, yet this ancient balancing trick keeps their heads startlingly steady in motion
science.summary: Postural control is a fundamental behavior for most animals, and head stability in particular plays a crucial role in achieving stable sensory input, accurate spatial perception, and efficient motor output. Tetrapods, including humans, possess a "neck"—a structure that separates the skull from the trunk skeleton—and stabilize their head in space by contracting and relaxing neck muscles in response to body tilt. This reflexive mechanism is known as the vestibulo-collic reflex.

science.title: Deep‑sea sponges survive in complete darkness in ways we didn't know before
science.summary: When we think of marine life, we usually picture colorful coral reefs or dense seaweed forests filled with fish and other critters. The ocean that comes to mind is the one touched by sunlight.

science.title: Mysterious acids keep bacteria rod-shaped by restraining rogue enzyme, experiments reveal
science.summary: Researchers have discovered how acids on the surface of bacteria give these microscopic organisms their characteristic "rod" shape—by keeping an enzyme at bay that would otherwise turn the cylindrical cells into shape-shifting blobs.

science.title: National Institutes of Health Nutrition Education Challenge
science.summary: NASA’s Center of Excellence for Collaborative Innovation (CoECI) assists in the use of crowdsourcing across the federal government. CoECI’s NASA Tournament Lab offers the contract capability to run external crowdsourced challenges on behalf of NASA and other agencies. The National Institutes of Health (NIH) Office of Nutrition Research (ONR) invites U.S.-based, accredited, non-profit academic institutions […]

science.title: Could aliens ever visit Earth? An aerospace scientist unpacks the challenges of interstellar spaceflight
science.summary: On May 22, 2026, the Pentagon released a second batch of previously classified photos and videos showing what appear to be unexplained flying objects. These file dumps were the culmination of a process that was set in motion back in July 2023, when a group of government whistleblowers testified before Congress that the U.S. government was secretly in possession of extraterrestrial spacecraft and suspected alien body parts.

science.title: Quantum teleportation carries microwave states at temperatures up to 4 K, beating classical limit
science.summary: A growing number of quantum engineers worldwide have been trying to realize large-scale quantum networks, which consist of several connected quantum computers or devices that share information with each other. The successful realization of these networks could potentially pave the way for the realization of new high-speed and secure communication systems, or even of a quantum version of the internet.

science.title: Your own personal Farmville: This VR greenhouse lets users monitor crops remotely
science.summary: You've probably heard of Stardew Valley or Farmville, video games where you manage a virtual farm. Now, what if you could monitor real plants from the comfort of your home? Thanks to new research at Binghamton University, State University of New York, that's becoming a reality.

science.title: Fire salamanders reveal hidden turquoise glow when exposed to UV radiation
science.summary: An international research team has discovered that the fire salamander (Salamandra salamandra) is biofluorescent. A study published in the journal Royal Society Open Science shows that the salamander reflects turquoise light when exposed to ultraviolet radiation. This phenomenon had gone unnoticed until now, despite decades of research on this amphibian species.

science.title: Fake predator eyes scare off seabirds until they learn they're not real
science.summary: In Danish fisheries, fish-eating seabirds are a menace. They often swoop down and feed on fish trapped in nets, which can hit profits and also sometimes lead to the accidental death of the birds. So scientists deployed an unusual-looking, non-lethal method to deter them. It worked, but not for long, as they discuss in a paper published in the journal Royal Society Open Science.

science.title: Tiny “sesame” sea slug discovered in Taiwan turns out to be a new species
science.summary: A sea slug smaller than a sesame seed has turned up in Taiwan’s coastal waters — and it’s so tiny and unusual that scientists realized they had discovered a completely new species. Named Thecacera sesama after its black-and-yellow “sesame-like” appearance, the translucent nudibranch was first spotted during a casual dive and later identified with help from a sea slug expert on Facebook.

science.title: 'Hook-up culture' on dating apps harming men's body image
science.summary: Gay dating apps are exposing men to intense pressure to look sexually desirable, fueling body dissatisfaction and low self-esteem, with some users describing the experience as feeling like they are "selling their body" rather than forming genuine connections.

science.title: NASA’s Webb telescope discovers a planet where rock clouds vanish every night
science.summary: A giant planet nearly 700 light-years away has a bizarre daily weather cycle where mineral clouds appear every morning and vanish by nightfall. Using the James Webb Space Telescope, astronomers discovered that WASP-94A b’s mornings are filled with clouds made of rock-like minerals, while its evenings are surprisingly clear. The finding gave scientists their clearest look yet into the planet’s atmosphere and revealed it’s far more Jupiter-like than previously believed.

science.title: Scientists discover a strange hidden state in “sandwich” molecules
science.summary: Scientists have uncovered a strange hidden structure formed during the creation of metallocenes, a class of sandwich-like molecules used in everything from catalysis to medicine. The newly characterized intermediate features a rare “double ring-slip,” where both carbon rings partially detach from the metal atom. By finally observing this fleeting state, researchers gained fresh insight into how these molecules assemble and transform.

science.title: Einstein’s “wormhole” may actually reveal a hidden mirror of time
science.summary: What if wormholes were never cosmic tunnels at all? New research suggests Einstein and Rosen’s famous “bridge” may actually reveal something even stranger: time itself could flow in two directions at once. Instead of connecting distant places in space, these bridges may connect mirror versions of time deep inside quantum physics, potentially solving the long-standing black hole information paradox and hinting that our universe existed before the Big Bang.

environment.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
environment.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

environment.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
environment.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

environment.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
environment.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

environment.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
environment.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

environment.title: Lake Coatepeque
environment.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

environment.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
environment.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

environment.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
environment.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

environment.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
environment.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

mix_items.title: Cristian Măcelaru, Cristian Mandeal și Corul Madrigal, pe scena Sălii Radio în cadrul Festivalului Internațional ISCM World New Music Days 2026 – „Columna Infinită”
mix_items.summary: Între 27 și 29 mai 2026, Sala Radio din București devine una dintre scenele centrale ale Festivalului Internațional ISCM World New Music Days 2026 – „Columna Infinită”, cel mai important… Articolul Cristian Măcelaru, Cristian Mandeal și Corul Madrigal, pe scena Sălii Radio în cadrul Festivalului Internațional ISCM World New Music Days 2026 – „Columna Infinită” apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.summary: Creșterea prețurilor la energie, dezvoltarea pieței prosumatorilor și interesul tot mai mare pentru independență energetică determină tot mai multe companii și persoane fizice să investească în sisteme fotovoltaice și soluții… Articolul Sistemele fotovoltaice și bateriile de stocare pot fi achiziționate și în leasing, prin soluțiile de finanțare oferite de E-acumulatori.ro apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.summary: Trupa Jack of all Trades a lansat „Live Rooftop Session – Piața Romană”, un material înregistrat și filmat pe acoperișul clădirii Magheru One, București, care aduce într-o formulă live piesele de pe… Articolul Jack of all Trades aduce albumul „Fiasco Milenial” deasupra Bucureștiului, printr-o sesiune live pe Magheru One apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: Singura investiție privată făcută în ultimii 35 de ani într-un domeniu din România nu a putut fi premiată
mix_items.summary: Regizoarea Chris-Simion Mercurian a reușit să construiască, în București, singurul teatru nou privat ridicat de la zero în România, după căderea comunismului. În septembrie 2025, teatrul a fost inaugurat.  Acum câteva zile Uniunea Teatrală din România (UNITER) a decernat zeci de premii pentru anul 2025. Teatrul nou nu a existat. Pe  19 septembrie 2025, în …

mix_items.title: Pentru Fundația Motivation, duminica trecută a fost despre fapte bune și, mai ales, despre o echipă unită în jurul unui scop dedicat binelui.
mix_items.summary: #TeamMotivation a alergat ca să schimbe în bine viețile copiilor și adulților cu dizabilități care au nevoie de sprijin în încercarea lor de a-și recăpăta independența. Evenimentul organizat de Fundația Comunitară Galați a adunat ambasadori a zeci de cauze pentru care alergătorii participanți au făcut tot posibilul să ajungă la finish. Pe lângă emoțiile și încurajările care au condimentat cursele din cadrul maratonului, pentru Fundația Motivation evenimentul a arătat așa: 14 utilizatori de scaun rulant au luat startul... mix_items.summary: „Extravaganța vocală” va avea loc în perioada 12–13 septembrie 2026 la Sala Palatului, marchează cea de-a doua ediție a Festivalului Masters of Classic și va pune în lumina reflectoarelor vedete… Articolul Masters of Classic (12–13 septembrie 2026) revine la București: o sărbătoare a operei și muzicii clasice apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

Brașovul a devenit, pentru câteva ore, … Read More The post Brașovul a devenit capitala tinerilor antreprenori din România la Arena Urșilor Junior 2026 appeared first on Stiri pozitive .

mix_items.summary: Ca și în alte situații similare, cei din Poliția Română au avut o reacție doar de fațadă după derapajul la adresa presei al purtătorului de cuvânt Georgian Drăgan. În loc să fie demis, cum ar fi fost firesc, el a fost doar mutat dintr-o parte în alta a ministerului de Interne. Ba chiar nu exagerăm […] The post Tipic! În loc să-l demită, Poliția l-a mutat pe alt post pe purtătorul de cuvânt: de pe buca dreaptă a lui Predoiu pe cea stângă appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.summary: Andrew și Tristan Tate s-au mișcat extrem de rapid. La scurt timp după ce au ajuns în Cluj, cei doi frați au reușit să cumpere clădirea fostului hotel Continental, aflată chiar în kilometrul zero al orașului, iar planurile lor sunt de-a dreptul mărețe. „Vom face aici cel mai luxos bordel din România”, a anunțat Andrew […] The post Frații Tate au cumpărat hotelul Continental din Cluj și îl vor transforma în bordel appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.summary: București, 20 mai 2026: Fundația World Vision România, împreună cu Liceul Agricol „Sandu Aldea” din Călărași, a inaugurat o seră... mix_items.title: The strange quantum property of tomorrow's insulator
mix_items.summary: Ultra-fast data transfer and superconductivity: Quantum materials offer significant technological prospects—if we can understand them at the atomic scale. A team from the University of Geneva (UNIGE), in collaboration with the University of Salerno, the Institute of Materials Science of Barcelona, and the National Research Council of Italy, has succeeded in observing the "quantum metric" in a topological insulator—a unique geometric property of these materials, which conduct electricity only on their surface.

mix_items.summary: Ionuț, elev în clasa a XI-a la un liceu din Municipiul Calafat, pasionat de muzică, cu un glas folcloric aparte,... Articolul Când dorința de a face bine este mai puternică decât emoțiile: Tiana și dragostea ei pentru voluntariat apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Blacks, Hispanics and Asians use asthma inhalers less than whites, study finds
mix_items.summary: Despite guidelines recommending daily controller inhalers as the best treatment for asthma, new UCLA-led research finds that Blacks, Hispanics and Asians use them less than whites, suggesting that socioeconomic factors and lack of access to specialty care are at the root of this gap.

Articolul Schimbările mari încep cu pași mici: povestea lui Robert și drumul lui către advocacy la nivel internațional apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: The impact of nanoplastics on neurons may depend on their size
mix_items.summary: Smaller plastic particles have more effects on neurons, the key information processing cells of the brain, new research from the University of Eastern Finland shows. In the study, neuronal cells were exposed to polystyrene nanoplastics at low doses to study subtle changes.

Suntem echipa de la Telefonul Vârstnicului, serviciu social licențiat, singura linie telefonică gratuită și confidențială, accesibilă la nivel național, dedicată persoanelor vârstnice, ce oferă consiliere socială și alinarea singurătății. În fiecare an suntem sunați de peste 5,000... Post-ul Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București! apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: Simple blood test could lead to personalized lung cancer treatment
mix_items.summary: A single blood test could help doctors predict how lung cancer patients will respond to treatment before therapy begins, researchers have found. University of Queensland-led research focused on non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC), the most common form of the disease, and showed how analyzing proteins in a blood sample could support earlier and better-informed treatment decisions.

mix_items.summary: Un drum construit prin educație Simona este elevă în clasa a XI-a, la specializarea Tehnician în industria alimentară, la un... Articolul Simona, eleva care descoperă viitorul industriei alimentare pas cu pas apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.summary: București, aprilie 2026 – Într-o lume în care cererea de hrană crește accelerat, iar presiunea asupra resurselor naturale este tot... Articolul Agricultura viitorului: World Vision România sprijină elevii din liceele agricole să devină fermierii erei digitale apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Women's faces outrank men's in attractiveness across cultures, global study shows
mix_items.summary: Why are women considered the "fairer sex" in humans, when in most animal species it is males that display the more elaborate and visually striking traits? This question has intrigued researchers since Darwin. A new large-scale study led by the Max Planck Institute for Empirical Aesthetics (MPIEA) in Frankfurt now provides clear empirical evidence for the first time. The findings have just been published in Proceedings of the Royal Society B.

mix_items.title: Psiholog
mix_items.summary: Locație: București | Nivel carieră: Experiență de minimum 1 an Candidatul Ideal: Ești tu vocea de care au nevoie? Un copil are nevoie de iubire, siguranță și încredere pentru a... The post Psiholog appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: Students Build Moon Robots for NASA’s 2026 Lunabotics Challenge
mix_items.summary: Katherine Rauscher of Michigan Technological University prepares her team’s prototype lunar robot for its turn during the finals for NASA’s 2026 Lunabotics Challenge competition on Tuesday, May 19, 2026, at the Kennedy Space Center Visitor Complex in Florida. Forty-seven teams from around the U.S. designed and built remote-controlled robots capable of traversing challenging lunar terrain while […]

mix_items.title: Advancing detection of genome-edited crops in food mixtures
mix_items.summary: Researchers from Sciensano, partner of the DARWIN project, have published a new paper in npj Science of Food addressing one of the key scientific and regulatory challenges linked to genome-edited (GE) organisms, their reliable detection and identification in complex food mixtures.

mix_items.title: New studies describe how immune modulation can effectively combat Valley Fever
mix_items.summary: Valley Fever, technically known as Coccidioidomycosis, is a dust-borne fungal infection that occurs in dry regions like the southwestern US and is proliferating in California and Arizona. California alone spends $1 billion a year on treatment.

mix_items.title: Natural trans fats in dairy do not raise heart disease risk
mix_items.summary: Trans fats found naturally in dairy foods such as milk, butter, and cheese do not increase the risk of heart disease or type 2 diabetes, a new study has found. Researchers analyzed evidence from 22 studies involving thousands of people across Europe, Canada, and the United States and found that natural trans fats behave very differently in the body from the industrial kind. Unlike industrial trans fats, which are strongly linked to heart disease, those found in dairy appear to pose no risk.

mix_items.title: Webinar 6/17: Discover, Access, and Task Commercial Data with NASA’s Satellite Data Explorer
mix_items.summary: Learn how to use the Satellite Data Explorer to search, access, and task commercial Earth Observation data.

mix_items.title: Coral study could help explain infertility and ovarian cancer by decoding cilia-driven fluid flows
mix_items.summary: A study by researchers at The University of Manchester, carried out alongside the Universities of Melbourne and Copenhagen, could hold the key to understanding the causes of long-term health problems, such as infertility and ovarian cancer.

mix_items.title: Planes, trains and pandemics: Lessons from COVID‑19 about travel risks posed by hantavirus and Ebola
mix_items.summary: International travel volumes have now fully recovered from the downturn during the COVID-19 pandemic. With the upcoming World Cup poised to drive a surge in Canadian tourism, recent hantavirus and Ebola virus outbreaks remind us of the need to effectively manage travel-related public health risks in a world on the move.

mix_items.title: Stress before conception may reprogram sperm and boost male offspring growth
mix_items.summary: Research from the University of Colorado Anschutz suggests that stress experienced by a father before conception may influence an offspring's growth by altering small molecular signals in sperm. The study, published in iScience, focuses on a tiny stress-responsive molecule called let 7f 5p, found in sperm. This molecule increases under stress. Researchers found that it may help shape how an embryo develops in its earliest stages, which can later influence growth after birth.

mix_items.title: Droplet-like clusters reveal new control switch for major drug-target receptors
mix_items.summary: A study by researchers at Duke University School of Medicine identifies a new way that G protein–coupled receptors (GPCRs)—targets of roughly one-third of FDA-approved drugs—control signaling in cells. The paper is published in the journal Nature.

mix_items.title: Studies expose hidden toll of trauma recovery in NZ
mix_items.summary: Researchers from the University of Otago—Faculty of Medicine—Christchurch Ōtautahi are calling for improved long-term psychological support for survivors of major trauma after landmark studies reveal many patients in Aotearoa New Zealand are struggling to access appropriate follow-up care.

mix_items.title: Human monoclonal antibodies fight antimicrobial resistance during disease treatment
mix_items.summary: The overuse of antibiotics is increasingly leading to the emergence of infectious superbugs—dangerous bacteria that have developed antimicrobial resistance (AMR) and are therefore much harder, if not impossible, to eliminate from the body. In a review published in Trends in Immunology, researchers highlight the promise of using human monoclonal antibodies (mAbs) to treat patients more effectively and tackle AMR.

mix_items.title: Extreme trait values may trace to rare genes with outsized effects, analysis suggests
mix_items.summary: Researchers at the Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai have found evidence that people who fall at the extreme high or low ends of certain traits, such as cholesterol, blood glucose, height, and age at menopause, are more likely to have a simple genetic explanation than previously thought.

mix_items.title: Long COVID may affect 18 million Americans, doubling surveillance estimates
mix_items.summary: The true toll of long COVID may be double that of current estimates and hidden from current surveillance systems that rely on capturing diagnostic codes, according to new research led by Mass General Brigham. Investigators have used a novel AI algorithm to comb through medical records of nearly 460,000 patients with COVID-19 across 58 U.S. hospitals, finding that approximately one in six (roughly 16%) developed long COVID.

mix_items.title: Years after polyp removal, gut microbiome changes may still shape colorectal cancer risk
mix_items.summary: More than a decade after removal of an adenoma—a precancerous mass—from the colon, alterations to the gut microbiome and metabolites remain and may drive heightened risk of colorectal cancer (CRC), according to a study led by researchers at Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health. The work is published in Cell Host & Microbe.

mix_items.title: NASA’s Webb Reveals Black Hole That Formed Before Its Galaxy
mix_items.summary: Which comes first, the galaxy or the black hole? We don’t know, but scientists have long thought it could be the galaxy: Large stars within an existing galaxy consume their fuel and collapse to form black holes, which can gobble up surrounding material and merge over time to form more massive entities. But it’s hard […]

mix_items.title: Fish have no necks, yet this ancient balancing trick keeps their heads startlingly steady in motion
mix_items.summary: Postural control is a fundamental behavior for most animals, and head stability in particular plays a crucial role in achieving stable sensory input, accurate spatial perception, and efficient motor output. Tetrapods, including humans, possess a "neck"—a structure that separates the skull from the trunk skeleton—and stabilize their head in space by contracting and relaxing neck muscles in response to body tilt. This reflexive mechanism is known as the vestibulo-collic reflex.

mix_items.title: Study links eight heart-healthy habits to lower diabetes risk in postmenopausal women
mix_items.summary: Type 2 diabetes is often associated with excessive sugar intake, but lifestyle factors including body weight, exercise, and smoking habits can also influence risk. A new study published in Diabetology has found that greater adherence to eight habits for cardiovascular health is associated with lower rates of diabetes for postmenopausal women.

mix_items.title: Deep‑sea sponges survive in complete darkness in ways we didn't know before
mix_items.summary: When we think of marine life, we usually picture colorful coral reefs or dense seaweed forests filled with fish and other critters. The ocean that comes to mind is the one touched by sunlight.

mix_items.title: Mysterious acids keep bacteria rod-shaped by restraining rogue enzyme, experiments reveal
mix_items.summary: Researchers have discovered how acids on the surface of bacteria give these microscopic organisms their characteristic "rod" shape—by keeping an enzyme at bay that would otherwise turn the cylindrical cells into shape-shifting blobs.

mix_items.title: Probiotics may help prevent childcare gastrointestinal bugs
mix_items.summary: New research suggests probiotics could help reduce "gastro bugs" in children attending childcare and kindergartens—potentially easing the familiar cycle of gastro lurgies for parents and families.

mix_items.title: National Institutes of Health Nutrition Education Challenge
mix_items.summary: NASA’s Center of Excellence for Collaborative Innovation (CoECI) assists in the use of crowdsourcing across the federal government. CoECI’s NASA Tournament Lab offers the contract capability to run external crowdsourced challenges on behalf of NASA and other agencies. The National Institutes of Health (NIH) Office of Nutrition Research (ONR) invites U.S.-based, accredited, non-profit academic institutions […]

mix_items.title: Could aliens ever visit Earth? An aerospace scientist unpacks the challenges of interstellar spaceflight
mix_items.summary: On May 22, 2026, the Pentagon released a second batch of previously classified photos and videos showing what appear to be unexplained flying objects. These file dumps were the culmination of a process that was set in motion back in July 2023, when a group of government whistleblowers testified before Congress that the U.S. government was secretly in possession of extraterrestrial spacecraft and suspected alien body parts.

mix_items.title: How inflammation can worsen artery plaque and also hold it back
mix_items.summary: A new LMU study shows how different immune cells variously influence the formation of dangerous vascular deposits—and identifies miR-147 as a potential starting point for future therapies.

mix_items.title: KRAS degradation induces rapid lung cancer regression in preclinical mouse models
mix_items.summary: KRAS is one of the oncogenes most frequently altered in cancer, mutating in approximately one-third of lung adenocarcinomas. For decades, it was considered undruggable, until the recent approval of the first inhibitors against specific KRAS mutants. However, these treatments often lose efficacy over time due to the emergence of resistance. Now, a new study explores a different pharmacological strategy based on inducing tumor cells to degrade mutant KRAS themselves.

mix_items.title: Quantum teleportation carries microwave states at temperatures up to 4 K, beating classical limit
mix_items.summary: A growing number of quantum engineers worldwide have been trying to realize large-scale quantum networks, which consist of several connected quantum computers or devices that share information with each other. The successful realization of these networks could potentially pave the way for the realization of new high-speed and secure communication systems, or even of a quantum version of the internet.

mix_items.title: Climate change linked to rising antibiotic resistance in Salmonella
mix_items.summary: Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) is mainly driven by the overuse and misuse of antibiotics, which allows resistant bacteria to survive and spread. However, rising temperatures and changing rainfall patterns can influence how bacteria survive, mutate, and spread, potentially increasing the exchange of antibiotic resistance genes. While previous studies have linked higher temperatures to greater levels of resistant bacteria, global quantitative studies on this relationship have been limited.

mix_items.title: Your own personal Farmville: This VR greenhouse lets users monitor crops remotely
mix_items.summary: You've probably heard of Stardew Valley or Farmville, video games where you manage a virtual farm. Now, what if you could monitor real plants from the comfort of your home? Thanks to new research at Binghamton University, State University of New York, that's becoming a reality.

mix_items.title: Daily fruit juice may lower depression scores in four weeks, trial suggests
mix_items.summary: People who drink a glass of 100% fruit juice or a smoothie each day as part of the U.K.'s 5-a-day healthy eating guidance see improvements in their mental well-being, according to new research from Newcastle University, U.K.

mix_items.title: Fire salamanders reveal hidden turquoise glow when exposed to UV radiation
mix_items.summary: An international research team has discovered that the fire salamander (Salamandra salamandra) is biofluorescent. A study published in the journal Royal Society Open Science shows that the salamander reflects turquoise light when exposed to ultraviolet radiation. This phenomenon had gone unnoticed until now, despite decades of research on this amphibian species.

mix_items.title: Strength exercises improve young people's hip pain
mix_items.summary: Physiotherapist-led strength exercises improve hip pain in young people suffering hip joint impingements, new research shows. The La Trobe University study followed 154 participants over six months, comparing a targeted strengthening program with a standardized stretching program. Participants in the strength group were 2.3 times more likely to report perceived improvements in pain and had larger improvements in hip muscle strength compared to participants in the stretch group.

mix_items.title: Fake predator eyes scare off seabirds until they learn they're not real
mix_items.summary: In Danish fisheries, fish-eating seabirds are a menace. They often swoop down and feed on fish trapped in nets, which can hit profits and also sometimes lead to the accidental death of the birds. So scientists deployed an unusual-looking, non-lethal method to deter them. It worked, but not for long, as they discuss in a paper published in the journal Royal Society Open Science.

mix_items.title: Tiny “sesame” sea slug discovered in Taiwan turns out to be a new species
mix_items.summary: A sea slug smaller than a sesame seed has turned up in Taiwan’s coastal waters — and it’s so tiny and unusual that scientists realized they had discovered a completely new species. Named Thecacera sesama after its black-and-yellow “sesame-like” appearance, the translucent nudibranch was first spotted during a casual dive and later identified with help from a sea slug expert on Facebook.

mix_items.title: 'Hook-up culture' on dating apps harming men's body image
mix_items.summary: Gay dating apps are exposing men to intense pressure to look sexually desirable, fueling body dissatisfaction and low self-esteem, with some users describing the experience as feeling like they are "selling their body" rather than forming genuine connections.

mix_items.title: Why you wake up so tired after vivid dreams
mix_items.summary: Some mornings when you wake up, your head is fuzzy, your body is heavy, and you don't feel rested. It felt like you were dreaming all night. But did all that dreaming actually wear you out? Let's look at what the science says.

mix_items.title: New laser heat treatment could stop blindness before it starts
mix_items.summary: A new experimental treatment could finally offer hope for millions of people with dry age-related macular degeneration — one of the leading causes of blindness in older adults. Researchers at Aalto University discovered a way to gently heat tissue at the back of the eye using near-infrared light, triggering the cells’ natural “cleanup and repair” systems before major damage occurs.

mix_items.title: NASA’s Webb telescope discovers a planet where rock clouds vanish every night
mix_items.summary: A giant planet nearly 700 light-years away has a bizarre daily weather cycle where mineral clouds appear every morning and vanish by nightfall. Using the James Webb Space Telescope, astronomers discovered that WASP-94A b’s mornings are filled with clouds made of rock-like minerals, while its evenings are surprisingly clear. The finding gave scientists their clearest look yet into the planet’s atmosphere and revealed it’s far more Jupiter-like than previously believed.

mix_items.title: The English restaurant turning hospitality on its head
mix_items.summary: At a pay-as-you-can restaurant in Stroud, radical hospitality and good food are bringing strangers together The post The English restaurant turning hospitality on its head appeared first on Positive News .

mix_items.title: The world’s largest museum of illustration is about to open its doors
mix_items.summary: British illustrator Quentin Blake has established a new museum dedicated solely to his craft, aiming to uplift other artists The post The world’s largest museum of illustration is about to open its doors appeared first on Positive News .

mix_items.title: Surprising study finds beef doesn’t worsen blood sugar or diabetes risk
mix_items.summary: A new clinical trial suggests that eating beef every day may not be as risky for people with prediabetes as many assume. Researchers found that adults who ate 6–7 ounces of beef daily for a month showed no worsening in blood sugar control, insulin function, inflammation, or other key markers linked to type 2 diabetes when compared to people eating poultry instead.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover a strange hidden state in “sandwich” molecules
mix_items.summary: Scientists have uncovered a strange hidden structure formed during the creation of metallocenes, a class of sandwich-like molecules used in everything from catalysis to medicine. The newly characterized intermediate features a rare “double ring-slip,” where both carbon rings partially detach from the metal atom. By finally observing this fleeting state, researchers gained fresh insight into how these molecules assemble and transform.

mix_items.title: Einstein’s “wormhole” may actually reveal a hidden mirror of time
mix_items.summary: What if wormholes were never cosmic tunnels at all? New research suggests Einstein and Rosen’s famous “bridge” may actually reveal something even stranger: time itself could flow in two directions at once. Instead of connecting distant places in space, these bridges may connect mirror versions of time deep inside quantum physics, potentially solving the long-standing black hole information paradox and hinting that our universe existed before the Big Bang.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover simple way to relieve arthritis pain without pills or surgery
mix_items.summary: A surprisingly simple walking tweak may offer new hope for millions living with knee osteoarthritis. In a year-long clinical trial, researchers found that slightly changing the angle of a person’s foot while walking reduced knee pain as effectively as common medications — and even slowed cartilage damage inside the joint.

mix_items.title: Scientists uncover surprising health benefits of watermelon
mix_items.summary: Studies suggest watermelon could be a hidden powerhouse for better health. Researchers found that people who eat watermelon tend to have higher-quality diets packed with more vitamins, fiber, and antioxidants — while consuming less added sugar and saturated fat. Another study showed watermelon juice may help protect blood vessel function and support heart health.

mix_items.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
mix_items.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
mix_items.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

mix_items.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
mix_items.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
mix_items.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

mix_items.title: Lake Coatepeque
mix_items.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
mix_items.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
mix_items.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

mix_items.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
mix_items.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

joke_ro.text: Mi-am facut lista de treburi. Acum am doua: lista si vinovatia.

satire_ro.title: Bucureștenii i-au spus lui Băluță să nu pună WC-uri în Parcul Trump, că se descurcă și fără
